european-debt-crisis-2009-onwards-still-.text: European Debt Crisis (2009 onwards, still unresolved?)

pageProps.body:
A collection of so called "stylized facts" in the empirical literature. This is listed in chronological order.

Note that because these are all stylized facts in economics, these are all controversial. Particularly controversial bits are emphasized.

The plus side of this controversy is that if something strange happens in the data, you can come here and pick and choose possible explanations.

Most stylized facts and events are noted here, even if they are a bit minor. The minor events may be useful for counterfactual analysis.

Energy Crisis (1973 and 1979)

A supply shock to crude oil, which is an input to almost everything. This resulted in stagflation, where these is stagnant growth, but also price inflation. The Keynesian model does not have a good solution to this, and the recommended "solution" is to simply do nothing.

The do nothing approach was essentially was happened - the world adjusted to higher input costs of oil by innovating and becoming more efficient in petroleum use. In particular, Japanese motors became much more efficient.

1973 Yom Kippur War

1979 Iranian Revolution

Great Moderation (1980s)

Ben's opinion is that the Great Moderation simply ocurred because central banks figured out/were allowed to finally function as intended (i.e. controlling inflation, employment and overall financial stability).

Stock Market Slump (1987)

Japan and West Germany pushed up interest rate, in turn presurring US to push up interest rates, triggering a sell of of shares.

It is believed that treasurer Keating set out to induce a recession (high unemployment) in order to control inflation.

In retrospect, the beneficial legacy of this recession was a lasting reduction in inflation.

Japanese Lost Decade (1990s)

The lost decade

Australian Recession (1991)

Australia's recession that "we had to have."

Asian Financial Crisis (1997)

Dot com bubble (2000)

Great Recession (2007/2008 GFC)

European Debt Crisis (2009 onwards, still unresolved?)

COVID19 (2019 - 2020)

Oil Price War

Russia - Saudi Arabia oil price war, oil prices declined more than 50%.

At one point, oil futures were negative value.

COVID19 (mid 2021 onwards, RIGHT NOW)

This is separated from COVID19 section, and is focused more on the reopening phase.

Inflation

Inflation has been noted to have gone crazy.

Issues:

  • Global supply chain issues
  • Shipping container issues
  • Semi-conductor shortages
  • Construction commodities (timber)
  • Crude oil price increase

Children

  1. COVID19
  2. Great Moderation
  3. Great Recession

noteIndex.title: PhD Homepage
noteIndex.body:
Welcome to my website!

This website has been built using Dendron, and each topic has been collated into collapsible sections on the left hand side. Below is a high level overview showing how each section is connected for ease of navigation.



Dendron has incredibly powerful search functionality due to good indexing, even outside of working in raw .md files. Simply use the search bar at the top of the website to find something.

## Quick Links

[[roadmap]]

[[Meeting]]

[[TODO]]

## To do List

Type up Bai and Ng assumptions

Investigate Bates et al Assumptions

Investigate consistency of using an estimated break point in conjunction with BNIC
This seems to be consistent using k_tilde that itself may be Op(1)

Fix up BP test for better interface (low priority)

Implement simulated Andrews exp and mean Wald/LM tests

- look into RSpectra package (supposedly faster eigen)
- not sure whether to rename this to SFM or not (lots of legacy code that needs to be changed)

Data
Fix and clean data for 
- AUMD (on hold, need to find date index)
- FREDQD
- CANMD (should be straightforward)
- CANQD
- UKMD (should be straightforward)

Investigate further sources of time series that could be used during COVID

Implement truly dynamic factor models (Forni) using Dynamic PCA
- BIG task
- read into dynamic PCA
- read into testing no. of dynamic factors
- do not believe there is a literature focusing on breaks in the dynamic setup
- implement dynamic PCA
- implement testing no of factors, (look into ICshocks functions and make this interface better)
- implement testing no of factors using AW test

Implement BKW standard test (non sequential)

Fix up BKW test
- implement a general plot method which plots *something* on the y axis, but most importantly, date on the x axis with estimated break dates, and each partition labelled with the no. of factors as determined by some consistent estimator

Fix up BE test
- so that there is a consistent interface for accessing this
